message.content:
can you use the github cli (gh) to dig into this pr: sta-1821-explore-different-forecast-horizons-4-8-12-weeks-model-config-adjustments, #203?

I want to add a column to include as part of the model! but I don't know how :( what table do I need to add this to?

content.text: I'll help you understand how to add a column to the model by examining PR #203. Let me start by looking at the PR details and changes.

input.description: Get PR #203 details and changed files

content.content:
{"additions":269,"body":"Change PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS from 4 to 8 to explore longer-range NRR prediction. Make repricing feature name dynamic via get_repricing_feature_name() so it adjusts automatically. Filter out rows with NULL targets for horizons beyond what the view pre-trims. Move TRAINING_END_DATE from Dec 20 to Dec 10 to expand the validation window (~18 days vs ~8), since observations from Dec 17+ are clean (both current and future MRR at post-repricing levels). Suffix all plot filenames with the horizon for side-by-side comparison.

# Testing Notes
Is there anything the reviewer should know?

# Deploy Notes
Notes regarding deployment of this PR. These should note any db migrations, Retool apps, Courier notifications, etc.

- [ ] I have performed a self-review of my own code
- [ ] I have commented my code, particularly in hard-to-understand areas
- [ ] I have added any deployment or testing notes above in the PR description

 # =============================================================================
 # FEATURE CONFIGURATION

@@ -67,14 +75,49 @@ def get_target_column() -> str:
 # - check_count_deposited_past_30_days: Checks deposited in last 30 days
 # - cumulative_*: Lifetime cumulative metrics (scans, checks, routing rules, etc.)
 # - cumulative_users/teams/tags/mailbox_locations: Org structure metrics (see hard-delete caveat in view SQL)
+# - login_days_past_15_days: Distinct days with at least one dashboard login in 15d.

+# - login_days_past_30_days: Same as above but 30d window. Range: 0-30.
+# - login_velocity_15d_30d: 2 * login_days_15d / login_days_30d — recent login
+#     acceleration. Same interpretation as scan velocity (1.0 = flat, >1 = increasing,
+#     <1 = declining, range [0, 2], 1.0 when denom=0).
+# - login_unique_users_past_15_days: Distinct users who logged in at least once in 15d.
+#     Measures breadth of team engagement. Complements login_days (frequency):
+#     1 user active 15 days vs 8 users active 15 days are very different signals.
+#     Same data availability caveat as login_days.
+# - login_unique_users_past_30_days: Same as above but 30d window.
+#
+#   LOGIN FEATURES — INTERPRETATION NOTE:
+#     More logins = higher predicted NRR. In the raw data, avg NRR rises
+#     monotonically from 0.95 (0 login days) to 1.05 (26-30 login days), and
+#     there are essentially zero churners among high-login customers.
+#
+#     However, the 1-way partial dependence plot (PDP) for login_days_past_30_days
+#     shows a counterintuitive *downward* slope. This is a known visualization

 FEATURE_COLUMNS = [

@@ -90,6 +133,11 @@ def get_target_column() -> str:

 ]

@@ -140,7 +188,7 @@ def get_continuous_features() -> list[str]:
 #
 # Any observation whose 4-week NRR horizon crosses this period has an inflated

@@ -223,6 +271,11 @@ def build_feature_query(
         "CASE WHEN ccm.mail_scans_past_60_days > 0 THEN 2.0 * ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days::FLOAT / ccm.mail_scans_past_60_days ELSE 1.0 END as scan_velocity_30d_60d",

+# Observations whose N-week NRR horizon crosses this period have inflated targets

 # Rather than excluding this data, we include it in the TRAINING set with a one-hot

+# (both mrr and mrr_in_{N}_weeks at the new price level, flag=0).
 #

 #
@@ -126,16 +127,17 @@
 #   |  TRAINING_START to TRAINING_END                        |  VAL_START to VAL_END    |
 #   |  2025-04-01      to 2026-01-01                         |  2026-01-01 to 2026-01-20|

-#   |  includes repricing period (Nov 17-Dec 30) with flag=1 |  all clean (flag=0)      |
+#   |  includes repricing period with flag=1                  |  all clean (flag=0)      |
 #   |  (used to fit model parameters)                        |  (early stopping)        |
 
 # Training period: used to fit model parameters
 TRAINING_START_DATE = "2025-04-01"
-TRAINING_END_DATE = "2025-12-20"
+TRAINING_END_DATE = "2025-12-10"
 
 # Validation period: used for early stopping to prevent overfitting.
-# Starts a few days after the price increase completed (Dec 15-16), so observations
-# have clean NRR ratios (both current and 4-week-ahead MRR at new prices).
+# Starts before the price increase (Dec 15-16), so validation includes some
+# repricing-flagged observations (Dec 10-16) plus clean ones (Dec 17+).
+# The model has the repricing feature available, so it can account for these.
 # Push TRAINING_END_DATE forward as more observed data becomes available.
 # Ends at the latest available data in view_churn_prediction_base.
 # NOTE: The view already excludes the last PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS weeks since
@@ -225,11 +227,13 @@ def load_training_data(
 
     with get_redshift_connection() as conn:
         # --- Resolve and validate validation_end ---
-        # view_churn_prediction_base already excludes the last PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS
-        # weeks (since mrr_in_N_weeks requires future data). Query the latest available

+        target_col = get_target_column()
         latest_date = pd.read_sql(

+            f" WHERE mrr > 0 AND {target_col} IS NOT NULL",
             conn,
         ).iloc[0, 0]
         # Exclusive end date: one day after the latest available observation
@@ -245,10 +249,9 @@ def load_training_data(
                 raise ValueError(

+                    f"The latest event_day with a non-NULL {PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}-week target is {latest_date}.\n"
+                    f"(mrr_in_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}_weeks requires future data "

@@ -492,7 +495,7 @@ def plot_feature_importance(
                 f"{imp:.3f}", va="center", fontsize=9)
 
     plt.tight_layout()
-    filepath = output_dir / "feature_importance.png"
+    filepath = output_dir / f"feature_importance_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}w.png"
     plt.savefig(filepath, dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
     plt.close()
 
@@ -518,6 +521,19 @@ def plot_average_predictions_by_feature(
     the feature is forced to each grid value while all other features stay at their observed
     values, isolating the model's learned relationship with that single feature.
 
+    WHEN AVERAGE PREDICTIONS AND PDPs DISAGREE:
+    If the average prediction curve goes *up* for a feature but the PDP goes *down* (or
+    vice versa), the most likely explanation is correlated features. The average prediction
+    preserves the natural correlations between features (e.g., high login days → high
+    scans → high engagement), while the PDP breaks those correlations by holding everything
+    else fixed, potentially creating impossible feature combinations.
+
+    In this situation the average prediction plot is the better guide for "does the model
+    think this feature is good or bad?" — and is the right chart to share with
+    non-technical stakeholders. The PDP is useful for understanding model internals but
+    can be misleading when features are highly correlated. See the correlation matrix
+    heatmap (plot_correlation_matrix) to identify which feature pairs are affected.
+
     Args:
         model: Trained XGBoost model
         X: Feature matrix used for predictions
@@ -583,7 +599,7 @@ def plot_average_predictions_by_feature(
         ax.axhline(y=1.0, color="gray", linestyle="--", alpha=0.5, label="NRR=1 (retention)")
 
         plt.tight_layout()
-        filepath = output_dir / f"avg_prediction_{feature_name}.png"
+        filepath = output_dir / f"avg_prediction_{feature_name}_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}w.png"
         plt.savefig(filepath, dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
         plt.close()
 
@@ -606,6 +622,18 @@ def plot_partial_dependence_1way(
     while keeping all other features at their observed values, then averages the predictions.
     This isolates the model's learned effect of the feature.
 
+    CORRELATED-FEATURES CAVEAT: When two features are highly correlated (e.g.,
+    login_days_past_30_days and login_days_past_15_days, r > 0.95), the 1-way PDP
+    can be misleading. It creates feature combinations that never occur in practice
+    (e.g., 25 login days in 30d but only 1 in 15d), and the model's response to
+    these impossible inputs shouldn't be interpreted as "the effect of this feature."
+
+    If a PDP slope contradicts the average-prediction plot for the same feature,
+    check the correlation matrix — correlated features are the usual cause. Use
+    the 2-way PDP (which varies both correlated features together) to see the
+    true joint relationship. See plot_partial_dependence_2way_login() for an
+    example of this diagnostic.
+
     Args:
         model: Trained XGBoost model
         X: Feature matrix
@@ -676,7 +704,7 @@ def plot_partial_dependence_1way(
         ax.axhline(y=1.0, color="gray", linestyle="--", alpha=0.5, label="NRR=1 (retention)")
 
         plt.tight_layout()
-        filepath = output_dir / f"partial_dependence_{feature_name}.png"
+        filepath = output_dir / f"partial_dependence_{feature_name}_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}w.png"
         plt.savefig(filepath, dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
         plt.close()
 
@@ -697,6 +725,13 @@ def plot_partial_dependence_2way(
     Filtered to custom plan customers only. Uses sklearn's partial_dependence() to compute
     the 2D PDP grid, then plots as a filled contour with colorbar showing predicted NRR.
 
+    OVERFITTING CAVEAT: With only ~5K custom-plan observations, specific (day_number,
+    mail_scans) regions may contain just one or two identifiable customers. "Red valleys"
+    in the contour (e.g., day ~1000, scans 500+) likely reflect the model memorizing a
+    single customer's churn/contraction rather than a generalizable pattern. Treat
+    localized dips skeptically — they may disappear with more training data or with
+    tree-depth constraints.
+
     Args:
         model: Trained XGBoost model
         X: Feature matrix
@@ -759,7 +794,133 @@ def plot_partial_dependence_2way(
     ax.set_title(f"2-Way Partial Dependence: Custom Plan Only (n={n_custom:,})")
 
     plt.tight_layout()
-    filepath = output_dir / "partial_dependence_2way_day_number_x_mail_scans_custom.png"
+    filepath = output_dir / f"partial_dependence_2way_day_number_x_mail_scans_custom_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}w.png"
+    plt.savefig(filepath, dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
+    plt.close()
+
+    logger.info(f"  Saved: {filepath}")
+    return str(filepath)
+
+
+def plot_partial_dependence_2way_login(
+    model: xgb.XGBRegressor,
+    X: np.ndarray,
+    df: pd.DataFrame,
+    output_dir: Path,
+) -> str:
+    """Create a 2-way partial dependence contour plot: login_days_past_30_days vs login_days_past_15_days.
+
+    This plot exists to diagnose a known PDP artifact: the 1-way PDP for
+    login_days_past_30_days slopes *down*, contradicting the raw data (where
+    NRR monotonically increases with login days). The cause is extreme
+    correlation between login_days_30d, login_days_15d, and mail_scans_past_30d.
+    When the 1-way PDP forces login_days_30d high while holding login_days_15d
+    at its (low) observed value, the model interprets this as declining
+    engagement — a pattern that barely exists in reality (678 of 2.8M
+    observations).
+
+    The 2-way PDP varies both features jointly, showing the true relationship:

+    login_15d_idx = FEATURE_COLUMNS.index("login_days_past_15_days")

+        features=[(login_30d_idx, login_15d_idx)],
+        kind="average",
+        grid_resolution=50,
+    )

+    grid_15d = pdp_result["grid_values"][1]
+    pdp_values = pdp_result["average"][0]
+
+    mesh_30d, mesh_15d = np.meshgrid(grid_30d, grid_15d)
+
+    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))
+
+    contour_filled = ax.contourf(
+        mesh_30d, mesh_15d, pdp_values.T,
+        levels=20, cmap="RdYlGn",
+    )
+    contour_lines = ax.contour(
+        mesh_30d, mesh_15d, pdp_values.T,
+        levels=10, colors="black", linewidths=0.5, alpha=0.4,
+    )
+    ax.clabel(contour_lines, inline=True, fontsize=8, fmt="%.3f")

+    diag_vals = np.linspace(grid_30d.min(), grid_30d.max(), 100)

+
+def plot_correlation_matrix(
+    X: np.ndarray,

+    """Create a correlation matrix heatmap of all features.

+    vice versa), the most likely explanation is correlated features. The average prediction
+    preserves the natural correlations between features (e.g., high login days → high
+    scans → high engagement), while the PDP breaks those correlations by holding everything
+    else fixed, potentially creating impossible feature combinations.
+
+    In this situation the average prediction plot is the better guide for "does the model
+    think this feature is good or bad?" — and is the right chart to share with
+    non-technical stakeholders. The PDP is useful for understanding model internals but
+    can be misleading when features are highly correlated. See the correlation matrix
+    heatmap (plot_correlation_matrix) to identify which feature pairs are affected.
+
     Args:
         model: Trained XGBoost model
         X: Feature matrix used for predictions

         plt.tight_layout()
-        filepath = output_dir / f"avg_prediction_{feature_name}.png"
+        filepath = output_dir / f"avg_prediction_{feature_name}_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}w.png"

     This isolates the model's learned effect of the feature.
 
+    CORRELATED-FEATURES CAVEAT: When two features are highly correlated (e.g.,
+    login_days_past_30_days and login_days_past_15_days, r > 0.95), the 1-way PDP
+    can be misleading. It creates feature combinations that never occur in practice
+    (e.g., 25 login days in 30d but only 1 in 15d), and the model's response to

     Filtered to custom plan customers only. Uses sklearn's partial_dependence() to compute

+    OVERFITTING CAVEAT: With only ~5K custom-plan observations, specific (day_number,

+    plt.savefig(filepath, dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
+    plt.close()
+
+    logger.info(f"  Saved: {filepath}")

+
+def plot_partial_dependence_2way_login(
+    model: xgb.XGBRegressor,
+    X: np.ndarray,
+    df: pd.DataFrame,
+    output_dir: Path,
+) -> str:
+    """Create a 2-way partial dependence contour plot: login_days_past_30_days vs login_days_past_15_days.
+
+    This plot exists to diagnose a known PDP artifact: the 1-way PDP for
+    login_days_past_30_days slopes *down*, contradicting the raw data (where
+    NRR monotonically increases with login days). The cause is extreme
+    correlation between login_days_30d, login_days_15d, and mail_scans_past_30d.
+    When the 1-way PDP forces login_days_30d high while holding login_days_15d
+    at its (low) observed value, the model interprets this as declining
+    engagement — a pattern that barely exists in reality (678 of 2.8M
+    observations).
+
+    The 2-way PDP varies both features jointly, showing the true relationship:
+    NRR increases along the diagonal (both 15d and 30d high together).
+
+    Uses all plan types (the artifact is structural, not plan-specific).
+    """
+    logger.info("Generating 2-way partial dependence plot (login_days_30d x login_days_15d)...")
+
+    login_30d_idx = FEATURE_COLUMNS.index("login_days_past_30_days")
+    login_15d_idx = FEATURE_COLUMNS.index("login_days_past_15_days")
+
+    pdp_result = partial_dependence(
+        model,
+        X,
+        features=[(login_30d_idx, login_15d_idx)],
+        kind="average",
+        grid_resolution=50,
+    )
+
+    grid_30d = pdp_result["grid_values"][0]
+    grid_15d = pdp_result["grid_values"][1]
+    pdp_values = pdp_result["average"][0]
+
+    mesh_30d, mesh_15d = np.meshgrid(grid_30d, grid_15d)
+
+    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))
+
+    contour_filled = ax.contourf(
+        mesh_30d, mesh_15d, pdp_values.T,
+        levels=20, cmap="RdYlGn",
+    )
+    contour_lines = ax.contour(
+        mesh_30d, mesh_15d, pdp_values.T,
+        levels=10, colors="black", linewidths=0.5, alpha=0.4,
+    )
+    ax.clabel(contour_lines, inline=True, fontsize=8, fmt="%.3f")
+
+    # Add diagonal reference line: login_15d = login_30d / 2 (steady engagement)
+    diag_vals = np.linspace(grid_30d.min(), grid_30d.max(), 100)
+    ax.plot(diag_vals, diag_vals / 2, "k--", alpha=0.5, linewidth=1.5,
+            label="Steady engagement (15d = 30d / 2)")
+    ax.legend(loc="upper left")
+
+    fig.colorbar(contour_filled, ax=ax, label="Predicted NRR (partial dependence)")
+    ax.set_xlabel("login_days_past_30_days")
+    ax.set_ylabel("login_days_past_15_days")
+    ax.set_title(f"2-Way Partial Dependence: Login Days 30d vs 15d (n={len(X):,})")
+
+    plt.tight_layout()
+    filepath = output_dir / f"partial_dependence_2way_login_30d_x_15d_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}w.png"
+    plt.savefig(filepath, dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
+    plt.close()
+
+    logger.info(f"  Saved: {filepath}")
+    return str(filepath)
+
+
+def plot_correlation_matrix(
+    X: np.ndarray,
+    output_dir: Path,
+) -> str:
+    """Create a correlation matrix heatmap of all features.
+
+    Helps identify highly correlated feature pairs that can cause misleading
+    1-way partial dependence plots. Features with |r| > 0.7 should be
+    interpreted together via 2-way PDPs rather than individually.
+
+    Args:
+        X: Feature matrix (n_observations x n_features)
+        output_dir: Directory to save the plot
+
+    Returns:
+        Path to saved plot file
+    """
+    logger.info("Generating feature correlation matrix heatmap...")
+
+    corr_matrix = np.corrcoef(X, rowvar=False)
+    n_features = len(FEATURE_COLUMNS)
+
+    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(16, 14))
+
+    im = ax.imshow(corr_matrix, cmap="RdBu_r", vmin=-1, vmax=1, aspect="equal")
+
+    # Add correlation values as text annotations
+    for i in range(n_features):
+        for j in range(n_features):
+            val = corr_matrix[i, j]
+            # Only annotate off-diagonal cells with |r| >= 0.3
+            if i != j and abs(val) >= 0.3:
+                color = "white" if abs(val) > 0.7 else "black"
+                ax.text(j, i, f"{val:.2f}", ha="center", va="center",
+                        fontsize=6, color=color)
+
+    ax.set_xticks(range(n_features))
+    ax.set_yticks(range(n_features))
+    ax.set_xticklabels(FEATURE_COLUMNS, rotation=90, fontsize=7)
+    ax.set_yticklabels(FEATURE_COLUMNS, fontsize=7)
+
+    fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label="Pearson Correlation", shrink=0.8)
+    ax.set_title("Feature Correlation Matrix")
+
+    plt.tight_layout()
+    filepath = output_dir / f"correlation_matrix_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}w.png"
     plt.savefig(filepath, dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
     plt.close()

-    filepath = output_dir / "prediction_vs_actual.png"

+    saved_files.append(plot_partial_dependence_2way_login(model, X_val, val_df, output_dir))
+

+

     saved_files.append(plot_prediction_vs_actual(model, X_val, y_val, val_df, output_dir, metrics))

     saved_files.append(plot_prediction_vs_actual_with_calibration(

-    # 7. Prediction distribution by category
+    # 9. Prediction distribution by category
     saved_files.append(plot_prediction_distribution_by_category(model, X_val, y_val, output_dir))
 
-    # 8. NRR distribution by plan type

     saved_files.append(plot_nrr_distribution(y_val, val_df, output_dir))

content.content:
1→"""
     2→Shared configuration for NRR prediction model.
     3→
     4→This module defines feature columns, plan type encodings, the feature query builder,
     5→and other configuration that must be consistent between training (train_nrr_model.py)
     6→and prediction (predict_nrr.py) scripts.
     7→
     8→IMPORTANT: Any changes to features here require retraining the model.
     9→When adding a new feature:
    10→1. Add it to FEATURE_COLUMNS below
    11→2. Add the corresponding SQL expression to build_feature_query()
    12→"""
    13→
    14→# =============================================================================
    15→# PREDICTION HORIZON
    16→# =============================================================================
    17→
    18→# Prediction horizon in weeks. The underlying Redshift view (view_churn_prediction_base)
    19→# must have a corresponding mrr_in_N_weeks column. Currently N=4, 8, and 12 are
    20→# available in the view. To support other values, add the columns to
    21→# view_churn_prediction_base first, then change this constant.
    22→PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS = 8
    23→
    24→
    25→def get_target_column() -> str:
    26→    """Return the target column name for the configured prediction horizon.
    27→
    28→    E.g., PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS=4 -> 'mrr_in_4_weeks'
    29→    """
    30→    return f"mrr_in_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}_weeks"
    31→
    32→
    33→def get_repricing_feature_name() -> str:
    34→    """Return the repricing indicator feature name for the configured horizon.
    35→
    36→    E.g., PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS=8 -> 'is_repricing_in_8w_horizon'
    37→    """
    38→    return f"is_repricing_in_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}w_horizon"
    39→
    40→
    41→# =============================================================================
    42→# FEATURE CONFIGURATION
    43→# =============================================================================
    44→
    45→# Feature columns used for prediction, in the order expected by the model.
    46→#
    47→# Current features:
    48→# - day_number: Days with positive MRR (customer "age" as a paying customer)
    49→# - trial_days: Days spent on trial before converting (0 if no trial)
    50→# - is_plan_grow/scale/custom: One-hot encoded plan type
    51→# - is_primary_id_*: One-hot encoded USPS 1583 primary ID grouping
    52→# - is_annual_payment_cadence: 1 if customer pays annually, 0 otherwise. Annual payers
    53→#     churn at dramatically lower rates (e.g. grow monthly 5.0% vs annual 0.9%).
    54→# - mail_scans_past_30_days: Mail scans in last 30 days (engagement signal)
    55→# - scan_velocity_15d_30d: 2 * scans_15d / scans_30d — recent scan acceleration
    56→#     (1.0 = flat, >1 = increasing, <1 = declining, range [0, 2], 1.0 when denom=0)
    57→# - scan_velocity_30d_60d: 2 * scans_30d / scans_60d — medium-term scan trend
    58→#     (same interpretation as above)
    59→# - rescan_rate_past_30_days: 100 * rescans_30d / scans_30d — rescan rate as a
    60→#     percentage of total scan volume (0 when no scans).
    61→#     NOTE ON INTERPRETATION: Rescans happen when a scan is low quality and the
    62→#     customer requests a re-scan. Intuitively this seems like a negative signal
    63→#     (poor experience), but empirically higher rescan *rates* correlate with
    64→#     retention — likely because they proxy for active engagement. A customer who
    65→#     notices and requests a rescan is actively using the product. The raw count
    66→#     is normalized by scan volume to separate the "engagement" signal from the
    67→#     "frustration" signal: a few rescans out of many scans is different from
    68→#     many rescans out of few scans. As the model matures with more data, we may
    69→#     learn that very high rescan rates (indicating persistent quality issues)
    70→#     eventually predict churn, but moderate rates indicate healthy engagement.
    71→#     TRAIN/SERVE SKEW CAVEAT: Rescan data comes from audit logs that began
    72→#     April 9, 2025, with the first actual rescan on July 10, 2025. The early
    73→#     months of the training window (Apr–Jun) have zero rescans for everyone.
    74→#     Coverage is strongest for custom/enterprise plans (~15% of observations).
    75→# - check_count_deposited_past_30_days: Checks deposited in last 30 days
    76→# - cumulative_*: Lifetime cumulative metrics (scans, checks, routing rules, etc.)
    77→# - cumulative_users/teams/tags/mailbox_locations: Org structure metrics (see hard-delete caveat in view SQL)
    78→# - login_days_past_15_days: Distinct days with at least one dashboard login in 15d.
    79→#     Sourced from Segment identify calls on authentication. Counts distinct DAYS,
    80→#     not raw events (identify fires on every page load). Aggregated at org level.
    81→#     Range: 0-15. DATA AVAILABILITY: Segment instrumentation began mid-July 2024;
    82→#     data reliable from August 2024 onward. Earlier event_days will be 0.
    83→# - login_days_past_30_days: Same as above but 30d window. Range: 0-30.
    84→# - login_velocity_15d_30d: 2 * login_days_15d / login_days_30d — recent login
    85→#     acceleration. Same interpretation as scan velocity (1.0 = flat, >1 = increasing,
    86→#     <1 = declining, range [0, 2], 1.0 when denom=0).
    87→# - login_unique_users_past_15_days: Distinct users who logged in at least once in 15d.
    88→#     Measures breadth of team engagement. Complements login_days (frequency):
    89→#     1 user active 15 days vs 8 users active 15 days are very different signals.
    90→#     Same data availability caveat as login_days.
    91→# - login_unique_users_past_30_days: Same as above but 30d window.
    92→#
    93→#   LOGIN FEATURES — INTERPRETATION NOTE:
    94→#     More logins = higher predicted NRR. In the raw data, avg NRR rises
    95→#     monotonically from 0.95 (0 login days) to 1.05 (26-30 login days), and
    96→#     there are essentially zero churners among high-login customers.
    97→#
    98→#     However, the 1-way partial dependence plot (PDP) for login_days_past_30_days
    99→#     shows a counterintuitive *downward* slope. This is a known visualization
   100→#     artifact, not a model problem. PDP holds all other features at their
   101→#     observed values while sweeping login_days_30d. Because login_days_30d is
   102→#     extremely correlated with login_days_15d and mail_scans_past_30_days, the
   103→#     PDP creates impossible feature combinations: e.g., login_days_30d=25 but
   104→#     login_days_15d=1 looks like a customer who stopped logging in — a
   105→#     declining-engagement signal the model correctly penalizes.
   106→#
   107→#     The average-prediction plot (which preserves natural correlations) shows
   108→#     the true upward relationship and is the right chart to share with
   109→#     non-technical stakeholders. The 2-way PDP (login_30d × login_15d) also
   110→#     resolves the paradox by varying both correlated features together.
   111→#     See plot_partial_dependence_2way_login() in train_nrr_model.py.
   112→# - mrr_change_past_30d: Current MRR minus MRR 30 days ago (dollar change).
   113→#     Captures expansion/contraction momentum without peeking at the target.
   114→#     Positive = recent upgrade or expansion; negative = recent downgrade;
   115→#     zero = stable. For brand-new customers (no MRR 30 days ago), equals
   116→#     current MRR (positive — they just started paying).
   117→# - is_repricing_in_{N}w_horizon: 1 if the observation's N-week NRR horizon crosses
   118→#     the Dec 2025 price increase period. Always 0 at prediction time; allows the
   119→#     model to learn from contaminated training observations without bias.
   120→#     The feature name is derived from PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS via get_repricing_feature_name().
   121→FEATURE_COLUMNS = [
   122→    "day_number",
   123→    "trial_days",
   124→    "is_plan_grow",
   125→    "is_plan_scale",
   126→    "is_plan_custom",
   127→    "is_primary_id_usa",
   128→    "is_primary_id_international",
   129→    "is_primary_id_incomplete_1583",
   130→    "is_annual_payment_cadence",
   131→    "mail_scans_past_30_days",
   132→    "scan_velocity_15d_30d",
   133→    "scan_velocity_30d_60d",
   134→    "rescan_rate_past_30_days",
   135→    "check_count_deposited_past_30_days",
   136→    "login_days_past_15_days",
   137→    "login_days_past_30_days",
   138→    "login_velocity_15d_30d",
   139→    "login_unique_users_past_15_days",
   140→    "login_unique_users_past_30_days",
   141→    "cumulative_mail_scans",
   142→    "cumulative_check_count_deposited",
   143→    "cumulative_check_usd_deposited",
   144→    "cumulative_registered_agent_locations",
   145→    "cumulative_completed_1583_flows",
   146→    "cumulative_routing_rules",
   147→    "cumulative_users",
   148→    "cumulative_teams",
   149→    "cumulative_tags",
   150→    "cumulative_mailbox_locations",
   151→    "mrr_change_past_30d",
   152→    get_repricing_feature_name(),
   153→]
   154→
   155→# Plan types to encode as binary features. All other plan types become the reference category.
   156→# Based on data distribution: grow (~77%), scale (~10%), custom (~1%), other (~12%)
   157→PLAN_TYPES_TO_ENCODE = ["grow", "scale", "custom"]
   158→
   159→# Primary ID groupings to encode as binary features. Maps the primary_id_grouping
   160→# value from view_churn_prediction_base to the feature column name.
   161→# Reference category: "Unknown (Never Started 1583)" — the largest unknown group,
   162→# with a churn rate (~3.3%) close to the overall average.
   163→PRIMARY_ID_GROUPINGS_TO_ENCODE = {
   164→    "USA": "is_primary_id_usa",
   165→    "International": "is_primary_id_international",
   166→    "Unknown (Incomplete 1583)": "is_primary_id_incomplete_1583",
   167→}
   168→
   169→
   170→def get_continuous_features() -> list[str]:
   171→    """Return the subset of FEATURE_COLUMNS that are continuous (not binary indicators).
   172→
   173→    Used for partial dependence plots, which only make sense for continuous features.
   174→    Binary indicators (is_plan_*, is_primary_id_*) are excluded automatically.
   175→    """
   176→    return [f for f in FEATURE_COLUMNS if not f.startswith("is_")]
   177→
   178→
   179→# =============================================================================
   180→# PRICE INCREASE CONFIGURATION
   181→# =============================================================================
   182→
   183→# A significant price increase rolled out on Dec 15-16, 2025.
   184→# Total MRR jumped ~8% on Dec 15 alone ($1.22M -> $1.32M): 9,506 customers
   185→# repriced that day ($95.5K total increase), with another 994 on Dec 16 ($10.5K).
   186→# After Dec 16 the daily increases drop to 2-45 customers / $50-$2K per day,
   187→# consistent with normal organic plan changes rather than systematic repricing.
   188→#
   189→# Any observation whose 4-week NRR horizon crosses this period has an inflated
   190→# target (new-price MRR / old-price MRR), which looks like organic expansion
   191→# but is really repricing. The is_repricing_in_{N}w_horizon feature flags these
   192→# observations so the model can learn the adjustment during training and ignore
   193→# it at prediction time (where the flag is always 0).
   194→PRICE_INCREASE_START = "2025-12-15"
   195→PRICE_INCREASE_END = "2025-12-17"  # 99% of repricing completed by Dec 16
   196→
   197→
   198→# =============================================================================
   199→# TRAINING CONFIGURATION
   200→# =============================================================================
   201→
   202→# Hyperparameter grids to evaluate via grid search during training. The model
   203→# with the lowest validation RMSE wins. To skip a grid search dimension, use
   204→# a single value.
   205→LEARNING_RATE_GRID = [0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2]
   206→MAX_DEPTH_GRID = [2, 3, 4]
   207→
   208→
   209→# =============================================================================
   210→# FEATURE QUERY BUILDER
   211→# =============================================================================
   212→
   213→
   214→def build_feature_query(
   215→    include_target: bool = False,
   216→    date_start: str | None = None,
   217→    date_end: str | None = None,
   218→    latest_day_only: bool = False,
   219→) -> str:
   220→    """Build SQL query for loading customer features for NRR prediction.
   221→
   222→    This is the single source of truth for the feature query used by both
   223→    training and prediction scripts. Centralizing the column selection, joins,
   224→    and data quality filters here prevents the training and prediction queries
   225→    from drifting apart.
   226→
   227→    The is_plan_* and is_primary_id_* features are NOT selected here — they are
   228→    derived in Python via encode_plan_type() and encode_primary_id_grouping()
   229→    after the query runs, using the plan_type and primary_id_grouping columns.
   230→
   231→    Args:
   232→        include_target: If True, include the target column (e.g., mrr_in_4_weeks).
   233→            The column name is derived from PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS.
   234→        date_start: Start date filter (inclusive), e.g. '2025-04-01'.
   235→        date_end: End date filter (exclusive), e.g. '2025-11-01'.
   236→        latest_day_only: If True, filter to only the most recent event_day.
   237→
   238→    Returns:
   239→        Complete SQL query string.
   240→    """
   241→    # -- CTE (only needed for latest-day prediction) --
   242→    cte = ""
   243→    if latest_day_only:
   244→        cte = """WITH latest_day AS (
   245→        SELECT MAX(event_day) AS max_day
   246→        FROM public.view_churn_prediction_base
   247→        WHERE mrr > 0
   248→    )
   249→    """
   250→
   251→    # -- SELECT columns --
   252→    # Always include identity/metadata columns needed by callers
   253→    select_cols = [
   254→        "cpb.customer_id",
   255→        "cpb.event_day",
   256→        "cpb.mrr",
   257→    ]
   258→    if include_target:
   259→        target_col = get_target_column()
   260→        select_cols.append(f"cpb.{target_col}")
   261→
   262→    # Feature columns queried from SQL (is_plan_* and is_primary_id_* are derived in Python)
   263→    select_cols.extend([
   264→        "cpb.day_number",
   265→        "GREATEST(0, ccm.day_number_including_trial_days - cpb.day_number) as trial_days",
   266→        "cpb.plan_type",
   267→        "cpb.primary_id_grouping",
   268→        "CASE WHEN mrr.payment_cadence = 'year' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END as is_annual_payment_cadence",
   269→        "ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days",
   270→        "CASE WHEN ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days > 0 THEN 2.0 * ccm.mail_scans_past_15_days::FLOAT / ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days ELSE 1.0 END as scan_velocity_15d_30d",
   271→        "CASE WHEN ccm.mail_scans_past_60_days > 0 THEN 2.0 * ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days::FLOAT / ccm.mail_scans_past_60_days ELSE 1.0 END as scan_velocity_30d_60d",
   272→        "CASE WHEN ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days > 0 THEN 100.0 * ccm.rescans_past_30_days::FLOAT / ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days ELSE 0.0 END as rescan_rate_past_30_days",
   273→        "ccm.check_count_deposited_past_30_days",
   274→        "COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_15_days, 0) as login_days_past_15_days",
   275→        "COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_30_days, 0) as login_days_past_30_days",
   276→        "CASE WHEN COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_30_days, 0) > 0 THEN 2.0 * ccm.login_days_past_15_days::FLOAT / ccm.login_days_past_30_days ELSE 1.0 END as login_velocity_15d_30d",
   277→        "COALESCE(ccm.login_unique_users_past_15_days, 0) as login_unique_users_past_15_days",
   278→        "COALESCE(ccm.login_unique_users_past_30_days, 0) as login_unique_users_past_30_days",
   279→        "cpb.cumulative_mail_scans",
   280→        "cpb.cumulative_check_count_deposited",
   281→        "ccm.cumulative_check_usd_deposited",
   282→        "cpb.cumulative_registered_agent_locations",
   283→        "cpb.cumulative_completed_1583_flows",
   284→        "ccm.cumulative_routing_rules",
   285→        "ccm.cumulative_users",
   286→        "ccm.cumulative_teams",
   287→        "ccm.cumulative_tags",
   288→        "ccm.cumulative_mailbox_locations",
   289→        "cpb.mrr - COALESCE(mrr_lag.mrr, 0) as mrr_change_past_30d",
   290→        f"CASE WHEN cpb.event_day >= DATE '{PRICE_INCREASE_START}' - INTERVAL '{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS} weeks' AND cpb.event_day < DATE '{PRICE_INCREASE_END}' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END as {get_repricing_feature_name()}",
   291→    ])
   292→
   293→    select_clause = ",\n        ".join(select_cols)
   294→
   295→    # -- FROM / JOIN --
   296→    cross_join = "\n    CROSS JOIN latest_day" if latest_day_only else ""
   297→
   298→    from_clause = f"""FROM public.view_churn_prediction_base cpb
   299→    JOIN public.view_customer_cumulative_metrics ccm
   300→        ON cpb.customer_id = ccm.customer_id
   301→        AND cpb.event_day = ccm.event_day
   302→    JOIN public.view_mrr_by_customer_by_day mrr
   303→        ON cpb.customer_id = mrr.customer_id
   304→        AND cpb.event_day = mrr.event_day
   305→    LEFT JOIN public.view_mrr_by_customer_by_day mrr_lag
   306→        ON cpb.customer_id = mrr_lag.customer_id
   307→        AND cpb.event_day - 30 = mrr_lag.event_day{cross_join}"""
   308→
   309→    # -- WHERE --
   310→    # Data quality filters applied consistently at both training and prediction time:
   311→    where_conditions = [
   312→        # Only paying customers (trials excluded)
   313→        "cpb.mrr > 0",
   314→        # Exclude "early custom switch" observations: customers who temporarily started on
   315→        # grow/scale while their custom contract was being set up. day_number_for_nrr < 0
   316→        # during this period, and their MRR doesn't reflect the true contract value.
   317→        "mrr.day_number_for_nrr >= 0",
   318→        # Exclude 'no_base_product': customers with addon MRR but no base plan,
   319→        # typically a transient billing setup issue.
   320→        "cpb.plan_type != 'no_base_product'",
   321→    ]
   322→
   323→    if include_target:
   324→        # Exclude rows where the target hasn't been observed yet. The view
   325→        # populates mrr_in_N_weeks as NULL when future data doesn't exist.
   326→        # For 4-week horizon this is a no-op (view already trims ~4 weeks),
   327→        # but for 8- or 12-week horizons there's a gap of rows with NULL targets.
   328→        target_col = get_target_column()
   329→        where_conditions.append(f"cpb.{target_col} IS NOT NULL")
   330→
   331→    if date_start:
   332→        where_conditions.append(f"cpb.event_day >= '{date_start}'")
   333→    if date_end:
   334→        where_conditions.append(f"cpb.event_day < '{date_end}'")
   335→    if latest_day_only:
   336→        where_conditions.append("cpb.event_day = latest_day.max_day")
   337→
   338→    where_clause = "\n      AND ".join(where_conditions)
   339→
   340→    return f"""{cte}SELECT
   341→        {select_clause}
   342→    {from_clause}
   343→    WHERE {where_clause}
   344→    ORDER BY cpb.event_day, cpb.customer_id
   345→    """
   346→
   347→
   348→# =============================================================================
   349→# S3 CONFIGURATION
   350→# =============================================================================
   351→
   352→S3_BUCKET = "stable-ml-models"
   353→S3_MODEL_PREFIX = "nrr-prediction-model"
   354→S3_PREDICTIONS_PREFIX = "nrr-predictions"
   355→

"""
Shared configuration for NRR prediction model.

This module defines feature columns, plan type encodings, the feature query builder,
and other configuration that must be consistent between training (train_nrr_model.py)
and prediction (predict_nrr.py) scripts.

IMPORTANT: Any changes to features here require retraining the model.
When adding a new feature:
1. Add it to FEATURE_COLUMNS below
2. Add the corresponding SQL expression to build_feature_query()
"""

# =============================================================================
# PREDICTION HORIZON
# =============================================================================

# Prediction horizon in weeks. The underlying Redshift view (view_churn_prediction_base)
# must have a corresponding mrr_in_N_weeks column. Currently N=4, 8, and 12 are
# available in the view. To support other values, add the columns to
# view_churn_prediction_base first, then change this constant.
PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS = 8


def get_target_column() -> str:
    """Return the target column name for the configured prediction horizon.

    E.g., PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS=4 -> 'mrr_in_4_weeks'
    """
    return f"mrr_in_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}_weeks"


def get_repricing_feature_name() -> str:
    """Return the repricing indicator feature name for the configured horizon.

    E.g., PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS=8 -> 'is_repricing_in_8w_horizon'
    """
    return f"is_repricing_in_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}w_horizon"


# =============================================================================
# FEATURE CONFIGURATION
# =============================================================================

# Feature columns used for prediction, in the order expected by the model.
#
# Current features:
# - day_number: Days with positive MRR (customer "age" as a paying customer)
# - trial_days: Days spent on trial before converting (0 if no trial)
# - is_plan_grow/scale/custom: One-hot encoded plan type
# - is_primary_id_*: One-hot encoded USPS 1583 primary ID grouping
# - is_annual_payment_cadence: 1 if customer pays annually, 0 otherwise. Annual payers
#     churn at dramatically lower rates (e.g. grow monthly 5.0% vs annual 0.9%).
# - mail_scans_past_30_days: Mail scans in last 30 days (engagement signal)
# - scan_velocity_15d_30d: 2 * scans_15d / scans_30d — recent scan acceleration
#     (1.0 = flat, >1 = increasing, <1 = declining, range [0, 2], 1.0 when denom=0)
# - scan_velocity_30d_60d: 2 * scans_30d / scans_60d — medium-term scan trend
#     (same interpretation as above)
# - rescan_rate_past_30_days: 100 * rescans_30d / scans_30d — rescan rate as a
#     percentage of total scan volume (0 when no scans).
#     NOTE ON INTERPRETATION: Rescans happen when a scan is low quality and the
#     customer requests a re-scan. Intuitively this seems like a negative signal
#     (poor experience), but empirically higher rescan *rates* correlate with
#     retention — likely because they proxy for active engagement. A customer who
#     notices and requests a rescan is actively using the product. The raw count
#     is normalized by scan volume to separate the "engagement" signal from the
#     "frustration" signal: a few rescans out of many scans is different from
#     many rescans out of few scans. As the model matures with more data, we may
#     learn that very high rescan rates (indicating persistent quality issues)
#     eventually predict churn, but moderate rates indicate healthy engagement.
#     TRAIN/SERVE SKEW CAVEAT: Rescan data comes from audit logs that began
#     April 9, 2025, with the first actual rescan on July 10, 2025. The early
#     months of the training window (Apr–Jun) have zero rescans for everyone.
#     Coverage is strongest for custom/enterprise plans (~15% of observations).
# - check_count_deposited_past_30_days: Checks deposited in last 30 days
# - cumulative_*: Lifetime cumulative metrics (scans, checks, routing rules, etc.)
# - cumulative_users/teams/tags/mailbox_locations: Org structure metrics (see hard-delete caveat in view SQL)
# - login_days_past_15_days: Distinct days with at least one dashboard login in 15d.
#     Sourced from Segment identify calls on authentication. Counts distinct DAYS,
#     not raw events (identify fires on every page load). Aggregated at org level.
#     Range: 0-15. DATA AVAILABILITY: Segment instrumentation began mid-July 2024;
#     data reliable from August 2024 onward. Earlier event_days will be 0.
# - login_days_past_30_days: Same as above but 30d window. Range: 0-30.
# - login_velocity_15d_30d: 2 * login_days_15d / login_days_30d — recent login
#     acceleration. Same interpretation as scan velocity (1.0 = flat, >1 = increasing,
#     <1 = declining, range [0, 2], 1.0 when denom=0).
# - login_unique_users_past_15_days: Distinct users who logged in at least once in 15d.
#     Measures breadth of team engagement. Complements login_days (frequency):
#     1 user active 15 days vs 8 users active 15 days are very different signals.
#     Same data availability caveat as login_days.
# - login_unique_users_past_30_days: Same as above but 30d window.
#
#   LOGIN FEATURES — INTERPRETATION NOTE:
#     More logins = higher predicted NRR. In the raw data, avg NRR rises
#     monotonically from 0.95 (0 login days) to 1.05 (26-30 login days), and
#     there are essentially zero churners among high-login customers.
#
#     However, the 1-way partial dependence plot (PDP) for login_days_past_30_days
#     shows a counterintuitive *downward* slope. This is a known visualization
#     artifact, not a model problem. PDP holds all other features at their
#     observed values while sweeping login_days_30d. Because login_days_30d is
#     extremely correlated with login_days_15d and mail_scans_past_30_days, the
#     PDP creates impossible feature combinations: e.g., login_days_30d=25 but
#     login_days_15d=1 looks like a customer who stopped logging in — a
#     declining-engagement signal the model correctly penalizes.
#
#     The average-prediction plot (which preserves natural correlations) shows
#     the true upward relationship and is the right chart to share with
#     non-technical stakeholders. The 2-way PDP (login_30d × login_15d) also
#     resolves the paradox by varying both correlated features together.
#     See plot_partial_dependence_2way_login() in train_nrr_model.py.
# - mrr_change_past_30d: Current MRR minus MRR 30 days ago (dollar change).
#     Captures expansion/contraction momentum without peeking at the target.
#     Positive = recent upgrade or expansion; negative = recent downgrade;
#     zero = stable. For brand-new customers (no MRR 30 days ago), equals
#     current MRR (positive — they just started paying).
# - is_repricing_in_{N}w_horizon: 1 if the observation's N-week NRR horizon crosses
#     the Dec 2025 price increase period. Always 0 at prediction time; allows the
#     model to learn from contaminated training observations without bias.
#     The feature name is derived from PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS via get_repricing_feature_name().
FEATURE_COLUMNS = [
    "day_number",
    "trial_days",
    "is_plan_grow",
    "is_plan_scale",
    "is_plan_custom",
    "is_primary_id_usa",
    "is_primary_id_international",
    "is_primary_id_incomplete_1583",
    "is_annual_payment_cadence",
    "mail_scans_past_30_days",
    "scan_velocity_15d_30d",
    "scan_velocity_30d_60d",
    "rescan_rate_past_30_days",
    "check_count_deposited_past_30_days",
    "login_days_past_15_days",
    "login_days_past_30_days",
    "login_velocity_15d_30d",
    "login_unique_users_past_15_days",
    "login_unique_users_past_30_days",
    "cumulative_mail_scans",
    "cumulative_check_count_deposited",
    "cumulative_check_usd_deposited",
    "cumulative_registered_agent_locations",
    "cumulative_completed_1583_flows",
    "cumulative_routing_rules",
    "cumulative_users",
    "cumulative_teams",
    "cumulative_tags",
    "cumulative_mailbox_locations",
    "mrr_change_past_30d",
    get_repricing_feature_name(),
]

# Plan types to encode as binary features. All other plan types become the reference category.
# Based on data distribution: grow (~77%), scale (~10%), custom (~1%), other (~12%)
PLAN_TYPES_TO_ENCODE = ["grow", "scale", "custom"]

# Primary ID groupings to encode as binary features. Maps the primary_id_grouping
# value from view_churn_prediction_base to the feature column name.
# Reference category: "Unknown (Never Started 1583)" — the largest unknown group,
# with a churn rate (~3.3%) close to the overall average.
PRIMARY_ID_GROUPINGS_TO_ENCODE = {
    "USA": "is_primary_id_usa",
    "International": "is_primary_id_international",
    "Unknown (Incomplete 1583)": "is_primary_id_incomplete_1583",
}


def get_continuous_features() -> list[str]:
    """Return the subset of FEATURE_COLUMNS that are continuous (not binary indicators).

    Used for partial dependence plots, which only make sense for continuous features.
    Binary indicators (is_plan_*, is_primary_id_*) are excluded automatically.
    """
    return [f for f in FEATURE_COLUMNS if not f.startswith("is_")]


# =============================================================================
# PRICE INCREASE CONFIGURATION
# =============================================================================

# A significant price increase rolled out on Dec 15-16, 2025.
# Total MRR jumped ~8% on Dec 15 alone ($1.22M -> $1.32M): 9,506 customers
# repriced that day ($95.5K total increase), with another 994 on Dec 16 ($10.5K).
# After Dec 16 the daily increases drop to 2-45 customers / $50-$2K per day,
# consistent with normal organic plan changes rather than systematic repricing.
#
# Any observation whose 4-week NRR horizon crosses this period has an inflated
# target (new-price MRR / old-price MRR), which looks like organic expansion
# but is really repricing. The is_repricing_in_{N}w_horizon feature flags these
# observations so the model can learn the adjustment during training and ignore
# it at prediction time (where the flag is always 0).
PRICE_INCREASE_START = "2025-12-15"
PRICE_INCREASE_END = "2025-12-17"  # 99% of repricing completed by Dec 16


# =============================================================================
# TRAINING CONFIGURATION
# =============================================================================

# Hyperparameter grids to evaluate via grid search during training. The model
# with the lowest validation RMSE wins. To skip a grid search dimension, use
# a single value.
LEARNING_RATE_GRID = [0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2]
MAX_DEPTH_GRID = [2, 3, 4]


# =============================================================================
# FEATURE QUERY BUILDER
# =============================================================================


def build_feature_query(
    include_target: bool = False,
    date_start: str | None = None,
    date_end: str | None = None,
    latest_day_only: bool = False,
) -> str:
    """Build SQL query for loading customer features for NRR prediction.

    This is the single source of truth for the feature query used by both
    training and prediction scripts. Centralizing the column selection, joins,
    and data quality filters here prevents the training and prediction queries
    from drifting apart.

    The is_plan_* and is_primary_id_* features are NOT selected here — they are
    derived in Python via encode_plan_type() and encode_primary_id_grouping()
    after the query runs, using the plan_type and primary_id_grouping columns.

    Args:
        include_target: If True, include the target column (e.g., mrr_in_4_weeks).
            The column name is derived from PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS.
        date_start: Start date filter (inclusive), e.g. '2025-04-01'.
        date_end: End date filter (exclusive), e.g. '2025-11-01'.
        latest_day_only: If True, filter to only the most recent event_day.

    Returns:
        Complete SQL query string.
    """
    # -- CTE (only needed for latest-day prediction) --
    cte = ""
    if latest_day_only:
        cte = """WITH latest_day AS (
        SELECT MAX(event_day) AS max_day
        FROM public.view_churn_prediction_base
        WHERE mrr > 0
    )
    """

    # -- SELECT columns --
    # Always include identity/metadata columns needed by callers
    select_cols = [
        "cpb.customer_id",
        "cpb.event_day",
        "cpb.mrr",
    ]
    if include_target:
        target_col = get_target_column()
        select_cols.append(f"cpb.{target_col}")

    # Feature columns queried from SQL (is_plan_* and is_primary_id_* are derived in Python)
    select_cols.extend([
        "cpb.day_number",
        "GREATEST(0, ccm.day_number_including_trial_days - cpb.day_number) as trial_days",
        "cpb.plan_type",
        "cpb.primary_id_grouping",
        "CASE WHEN mrr.payment_cadence = 'year' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END as is_annual_payment_cadence",
        "ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days",
        "CASE WHEN ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days > 0 THEN 2.0 * ccm.mail_scans_past_15_days::FLOAT / ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days ELSE 1.0 END as scan_velocity_15d_30d",
        "CASE WHEN ccm.mail_scans_past_60_days > 0 THEN 2.0 * ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days::FLOAT / ccm.mail_scans_past_60_days ELSE 1.0 END as scan_velocity_30d_60d",
        "CASE WHEN ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days > 0 THEN 100.0 * ccm.rescans_past_30_days::FLOAT / ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days ELSE 0.0 END as rescan_rate_past_30_days",
        "ccm.check_count_deposited_past_30_days",
        "COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_15_days, 0) as login_days_past_15_days",
        "COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_30_days, 0) as login_days_past_30_days",
        "CASE WHEN COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_30_days, 0) > 0 THEN 2.0 * ccm.login_days_past_15_days::FLOAT / ccm.login_days_past_30_days ELSE 1.0 END as login_velocity_15d_30d",
        "COALESCE(ccm.login_unique_users_past_15_days, 0) as login_unique_users_past_15_days",
        "COALESCE(ccm.login_unique_users_past_30_days, 0) as login_unique_users_past_30_days",
        "cpb.cumulative_mail_scans",
        "cpb.cumulative_check_count_deposited",
        "ccm.cumulative_check_usd_deposited",
        "cpb.cumulative_registered_agent_locations",
        "cpb.cumulative_completed_1583_flows",
        "ccm.cumulative_routing_rules",
        "ccm.cumulative_users",
        "ccm.cumulative_teams",
        "ccm.cumulative_tags",
        "ccm.cumulative_mailbox_locations",
        "cpb.mrr - COALESCE(mrr_lag.mrr, 0) as mrr_change_past_30d",
        f"CASE WHEN cpb.event_day >= DATE '{PRICE_INCREASE_START}' - INTERVAL '{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS} weeks' AND cpb.event_day < DATE '{PRICE_INCREASE_END}' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END as {get_repricing_feature_name()}",
    ])

    select_clause = ",\n        ".join(select_cols)

    # -- FROM / JOIN --
    cross_join = "\n    CROSS JOIN latest_day" if latest_day_only else ""

    from_clause = f"""FROM public.view_churn_prediction_base cpb
    JOIN public.view_customer_cumulative_metrics ccm
        ON cpb.customer_id = ccm.customer_id
        AND cpb.event_day = ccm.event_day
    JOIN public.view_mrr_by_customer_by_day mrr
        ON cpb.customer_id = mrr.customer_id
        AND cpb.event_day = mrr.event_day
    LEFT JOIN public.view_mrr_by_customer_by_day mrr_lag
        ON cpb.customer_id = mrr_lag.customer_id
        AND cpb.event_day - 30 = mrr_lag.event_day{cross_join}"""

    # -- WHERE --
    # Data quality filters applied consistently at both training and prediction time:
    where_conditions = [
        # Only paying customers (trials excluded)
        "cpb.mrr > 0",
        # Exclude "early custom switch" observations: customers who temporarily started on
        # grow/scale while their custom contract was being set up. day_number_for_nrr < 0
        # during this period, and their MRR doesn't reflect the true contract value.
        "mrr.day_number_for_nrr >= 0",
        # Exclude 'no_base_product': customers with addon MRR but no base plan,
        # typically a transient billing setup issue.
        "cpb.plan_type != 'no_base_product'",
    ]

    if include_target:
        # Exclude rows where the target hasn't been observed yet. The view
        # populates mrr_in_N_weeks as NULL when future data doesn't exist.
        # For 4-week horizon this is a no-op (view already trims ~4 weeks),
        # but for 8- or 12-week horizons there's a gap of rows with NULL targets.
        target_col = get_target_column()
        where_conditions.append(f"cpb.{target_col} IS NOT NULL")

    if date_start:
        where_conditions.append(f"cpb.event_day >= '{date_start}'")
    if date_end:
        where_conditions.append(f"cpb.event_day < '{date_end}'")
    if latest_day_only:
        where_conditions.append("cpb.event_day = latest_day.max_day")

    where_clause = "\n      AND ".join(where_conditions)

    return f"""{cte}SELECT
        {select_clause}
    {from_clause}
    WHERE {where_clause}
    ORDER BY cpb.event_day, cpb.customer_id
    """


# =============================================================================
# S3 CONFIGURATION
# =============================================================================

S3_BUCKET = "stable-ml-models"
S3_MODEL_PREFIX = "nrr-prediction-model"
S3_PREDICTIONS_PREFIX = "nrr-predictions"

content.text:
Perfect! Now I understand the model structure. Based on the PR changes and the model configuration, here's how to add a new column to the NRR prediction model:

## Adding a Column to the Model

To add a new feature to the NRR prediction model, you need to modify **two places**:

### 1. Add to `FEATURE_COLUMNS` list (lines 121-153)
Add your new feature name to the `FEATURE_COLUMNS` list in `python_models/model_config.py`. This list defines all features the model expects in a specific order.

### 2. Add SQL expression to `build_feature_query()` (lines 263-291)  
Add the corresponding SQL expression to the `select_cols.extend([...])` section in the `build_feature_query()` function to actually compute your feature from the database.

### Example from the PR:
The PR added login-related features like this:

**In FEATURE_COLUMNS:**
```python
"login_days_past_15_days",
"login_days_past_30_days", 
"login_velocity_15d_30d",
"login_unique_users_past_15_days",
"login_unique_users_past_30_days",
```

**In build_feature_query():**
```python
"COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_15_days, 0) as login_days_past_15_days",
"COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_30_days, 0) as login_days_past_30_days", 
"CASE WHEN COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_30_days, 0) > 0 THEN 2.0 * ccm.login_days_past_15_days::FLOAT / ccm.login_days_past_30_days ELSE 1.0 END as login_velocity_15d_30d",
```

### Data Sources Available:
The query joins these tables, so you can reference columns from:
- `cpb` (view_churn_prediction_base) - main customer/event data
- `ccm` (view_customer_cumulative_metrics) - metrics and engagement data  
- `mrr` (view_mrr_by_customer_by_day) - MRR and payment data
- `mrr_lag` - MRR from 30 days ago

### Important Notes:
- **Order matters** - features must be in the same order in both places
- **Retraining required** - any feature changes require retraining the model
- **Handle NULLs** - use `COALESCE()` for nullable columns to avoid training issues
- **Document your feature** - add comments explaining what it measures and any caveats

What specific feature are you looking to add? I can help you with the exact SQL expression needed.

content.text:
Great question! Here are your options for adding the intercom conversation features to the model:

## Option 1: Build on top of PR #203 (Recommended)
This is cleanest since you want to extend the existing model work:

```bash
# Switch to the PR branch that has the model code
git checkout sta-1821-explore-different-forecast-horizons-4-8-12-weeks-model-config-adjustments

# Create a new branch from there for your intercom features  
git checkout -b add-intercom-conversation-features

# Make your changes to model_config.py
# (Add the 3 intercom features we discussed)

# When done, commit your changes
git add python_models/model_config.py
git commit -m "Add intercom conversation features to NRR model"

# Push your new branch
git push -u origin add-intercom-conversation-features

# Create a new PR that builds on PR #203
gh pr create --title "Add intercom conversation features to NRR model" --base sta-1821-explore-different-forecast-horizons-4-8-12-weeks-model-config-adjustments
```

## Option 2: Wait for PR #203 to merge, then branch from master
```bash
# Wait for PR #203 to be merged into master first
# Then switch to master and pull latest
git checkout master
git pull origin master

# Create your branch from the updated master
git checkout -b add-intercom-conversation-features

# Make changes and follow normal PR process to master
```

## Option 3: Branch from master and handle merge conflicts later
```bash
# Stay on master, create your own branch
git checkout master  
git checkout -b add-intercom-conversation-features

# You'd need to either:
# - Copy the model_config.py from PR #203 manually, or  
# - Add your features to the current master version and deal with merge conflicts when PR #203 merges
```

**I recommend Option 1** because:
- You get the latest model improvements from PR #203
- Your PR will be easier to review (just the intercom features)
- Less risk of merge conflicts
- You can test your features with the 8-week horizon setup

Which approach sounds best to you?

"""
Shared configuration for NRR prediction model.

This module defines feature columns, plan type encodings, the feature query builder,
and other configuration that must be consistent between training (train_nrr_model.py)
and prediction (predict_nrr.py) scripts.

IMPORTANT: Any changes to features here require retraining the model.
When adding a new feature:
1. Add it to FEATURE_COLUMNS below
2. Add the corresponding SQL expression to build_feature_query()
"""

# =============================================================================
# PREDICTION HORIZON
# =============================================================================

# Prediction horizon in weeks. The underlying Redshift view (view_churn_prediction_base)
# must have a corresponding mrr_in_N_weeks column. Currently N=4, 8, and 12 are
# available in the view. To support other values, add the columns to
# view_churn_prediction_base first, then change this constant.
PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS = 8


def get_target_column() -> str:
    """Return the target column name for the configured prediction horizon.

    E.g., PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS=4 -> 'mrr_in_4_weeks'
    """
    return f"mrr_in_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}_weeks"


def get_repricing_feature_name() -> str:
    """Return the repricing indicator feature name for the configured horizon.

    E.g., PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS=8 -> 'is_repricing_in_8w_horizon'
    """
    return f"is_repricing_in_{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS}w_horizon"


# =============================================================================
# FEATURE CONFIGURATION
# =============================================================================

# Feature columns used for prediction, in the order expected by the model.
#
# Current features:
# - day_number: Days with positive MRR (customer "age" as a paying customer)
# - trial_days: Days spent on trial before converting (0 if no trial)
# - is_plan_grow/scale/custom: One-hot encoded plan type
# - is_primary_id_*: One-hot encoded USPS 1583 primary ID grouping
# - is_annual_payment_cadence: 1 if customer pays annually, 0 otherwise. Annual payers
#     churn at dramatically lower rates (e.g. grow monthly 5.0% vs annual 0.9%).
# - mail_scans_past_30_days: Mail scans in last 30 days (engagement signal)
# - scan_velocity_15d_30d: 2 * scans_15d / scans_30d — recent scan acceleration
#     (1.0 = flat, >1 = increasing, <1 = declining, range [0, 2], 1.0 when denom=0)
# - scan_velocity_30d_60d: 2 * scans_30d / scans_60d — medium-term scan trend
#     (same interpretation as above)
# - rescan_rate_past_30_days: 100 * rescans_30d / scans_30d — rescan rate as a
#     percentage of total scan volume (0 when no scans).
#     NOTE ON INTERPRETATION: Rescans happen when a scan is low quality and the
#     customer requests a re-scan. Intuitively this seems like a negative signal
#     (poor experience), but empirically higher rescan *rates* correlate with
#     retention — likely because they proxy for active engagement. A customer who
#     notices and requests a rescan is actively using the product. The raw count
#     is normalized by scan volume to separate the "engagement" signal from the
#     "frustration" signal: a few rescans out of many scans is different from
#     many rescans out of few scans. As the model matures with more data, we may
#     learn that very high rescan rates (indicating persistent quality issues)
#     eventually predict churn, but moderate rates indicate healthy engagement.
#     TRAIN/SERVE SKEW CAVEAT: Rescan data comes from audit logs that began
#     April 9, 2025, with the first actual rescan on July 10, 2025. The early
#     months of the training window (Apr–Jun) have zero rescans for everyone.
#     Coverage is strongest for custom/enterprise plans (~15% of observations).
# - check_count_deposited_past_30_days: Checks deposited in last 30 days
# - cumulative_*: Lifetime cumulative metrics (scans, checks, routing rules, etc.)
# - cumulative_users/teams/tags/mailbox_locations: Org structure metrics (see hard-delete caveat in view SQL)
# - login_days_past_15_days: Distinct days with at least one dashboard login in 15d.
#     Sourced from Segment identify calls on authentication. Counts distinct DAYS,
#     not raw events (identify fires on every page load). Aggregated at org level.
#     Range: 0-15. DATA AVAILABILITY: Segment instrumentation began mid-July 2024;
#     data reliable from August 2024 onward. Earlier event_days will be 0.
# - login_days_past_30_days: Same as above but 30d window. Range: 0-30.
# - login_velocity_15d_30d: 2 * login_days_15d / login_days_30d — recent login
#     acceleration. Same interpretation as scan velocity (1.0 = flat, >1 = increasing,
#     <1 = declining, range [0, 2], 1.0 when denom=0).
# - login_unique_users_past_15_days: Distinct users who logged in at least once in 15d.
#     Measures breadth of team engagement. Complements login_days (frequency):
#     1 user active 15 days vs 8 users active 15 days are very different signals.
#     Same data availability caveat as login_days.
# - login_unique_users_past_30_days: Same as above but 30d window.
#
#   LOGIN FEATURES — INTERPRETATION NOTE:
#     More logins = higher predicted NRR. In the raw data, avg NRR rises
#     monotonically from 0.95 (0 login days) to 1.05 (26-30 login days), and
#     there are essentially zero churners among high-login customers.
#
#     However, the 1-way partial dependence plot (PDP) for login_days_past_30_days
#     shows a counterintuitive *downward* slope. This is a known visualization
#     artifact, not a model problem. PDP holds all other features at their
#     observed values while sweeping login_days_30d. Because login_days_30d is
#     extremely correlated with login_days_15d and mail_scans_past_30_days, the
#     PDP creates impossible feature combinations: e.g., login_days_30d=25 but
#     login_days_15d=1 looks like a customer who stopped logging in — a
#     declining-engagement signal the model correctly penalizes.
#
#     The average-prediction plot (which preserves natural correlations) shows
#     the true upward relationship and is the right chart to share with
#     non-technical stakeholders. The 2-way PDP (login_30d × login_15d) also
#     resolves the paradox by varying both correlated features together.
#     See plot_partial_dependence_2way_login() in train_nrr_model.py.
# - mrr_change_past_30d: Current MRR minus MRR 30 days ago (dollar change).
#     Captures expansion/contraction momentum without peeking at the target.
#     Positive = recent upgrade or expansion; negative = recent downgrade;
#     zero = stable. For brand-new customers (no MRR 30 days ago), equals
#     current MRR (positive — they just started paying).
# - is_repricing_in_{N}w_horizon: 1 if the observation's N-week NRR horizon crosses
#     the Dec 2025 price increase period. Always 0 at prediction time; allows the
#     model to learn from contaminated training observations without bias.
#     The feature name is derived from PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS via get_repricing_feature_name().
FEATURE_COLUMNS = [
    "day_number",
    "trial_days",
    "is_plan_grow",
    "is_plan_scale",
    "is_plan_custom",
    "is_primary_id_usa",
    "is_primary_id_international",
    "is_primary_id_incomplete_1583",
    "is_annual_payment_cadence",
    "mail_scans_past_30_days",
    "scan_velocity_15d_30d",
    "scan_velocity_30d_60d",
    "rescan_rate_past_30_days",
    "check_count_deposited_past_30_days",
    "login_days_past_15_days",
    "login_days_past_30_days",
    "login_velocity_15d_30d",
    "login_unique_users_past_15_days",
    "login_unique_users_past_30_days",
    "cumulative_mail_scans",
    "cumulative_check_count_deposited",
    "cumulative_check_usd_deposited",
    "cumulative_registered_agent_locations",
    "cumulative_completed_1583_flows",
    "cumulative_routing_rules",
    "cumulative_users",
    "cumulative_teams",
    "cumulative_tags",
    "cumulative_mailbox_locations",
    "mrr_change_past_30d",
    get_repricing_feature_name(),
]

# Plan types to encode as binary features. All other plan types become the reference category.
# Based on data distribution: grow (~77%), scale (~10%), custom (~1%), other (~12%)
PLAN_TYPES_TO_ENCODE = ["grow", "scale", "custom"]

# Primary ID groupings to encode as binary features. Maps the primary_id_grouping
# value from view_churn_prediction_base to the feature column name.
# Reference category: "Unknown (Never Started 1583)" — the largest unknown group,
# with a churn rate (~3.3%) close to the overall average.
PRIMARY_ID_GROUPINGS_TO_ENCODE = {
    "USA": "is_primary_id_usa",
    "International": "is_primary_id_international",
    "Unknown (Incomplete 1583)": "is_primary_id_incomplete_1583",
}


def get_continuous_features() -> list[str]:
    """Return the subset of FEATURE_COLUMNS that are continuous (not binary indicators).

    Used for partial dependence plots, which only make sense for continuous features.
    Binary indicators (is_plan_*, is_primary_id_*) are excluded automatically.
    """
    return [f for f in FEATURE_COLUMNS if not f.startswith("is_")]


# =============================================================================
# PRICE INCREASE CONFIGURATION
# =============================================================================

# A significant price increase rolled out on Dec 15-16, 2025.
# Total MRR jumped ~8% on Dec 15 alone ($1.22M -> $1.32M): 9,506 customers
# repriced that day ($95.5K total increase), with another 994 on Dec 16 ($10.5K).
# After Dec 16 the daily increases drop to 2-45 customers / $50-$2K per day,
# consistent with normal organic plan changes rather than systematic repricing.
#
# Any observation whose 4-week NRR horizon crosses this period has an inflated
# target (new-price MRR / old-price MRR), which looks like organic expansion
# but is really repricing. The is_repricing_in_{N}w_horizon feature flags these
# observations so the model can learn the adjustment during training and ignore
# it at prediction time (where the flag is always 0).
PRICE_INCREASE_START = "2025-12-15"
PRICE_INCREASE_END = "2025-12-17"  # 99% of repricing completed by Dec 16


# =============================================================================
# TRAINING CONFIGURATION
# =============================================================================

# Hyperparameter grids to evaluate via grid search during training. The model
# with the lowest validation RMSE wins. To skip a grid search dimension, use
# a single value.
LEARNING_RATE_GRID = [0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2]
MAX_DEPTH_GRID = [2, 3, 4]


# =============================================================================
# FEATURE QUERY BUILDER
# =============================================================================


def build_feature_query(
    include_target: bool = False,
    date_start: str | None = None,
    date_end: str | None = None,
    latest_day_only: bool = False,
) -> str:
    """Build SQL query for loading customer features for NRR prediction.

    This is the single source of truth for the feature query used by both
    training and prediction scripts. Centralizing the column selection, joins,
    and data quality filters here prevents the training and prediction queries
    from drifting apart.

    The is_plan_* and is_primary_id_* features are NOT selected here — they are
    derived in Python via encode_plan_type() and encode_primary_id_grouping()
    after the query runs, using the plan_type and primary_id_grouping columns.

    Args:
        include_target: If True, include the target column (e.g., mrr_in_4_weeks).
            The column name is derived from PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS.
        date_start: Start date filter (inclusive), e.g. '2025-04-01'.
        date_end: End date filter (exclusive), e.g. '2025-11-01'.
        latest_day_only: If True, filter to only the most recent event_day.

    Returns:
        Complete SQL query string.
    """
    # -- CTE (only needed for latest-day prediction) --
    cte = ""
    if latest_day_only:
        cte = """WITH latest_day AS (
        SELECT MAX(event_day) AS max_day
        FROM public.view_churn_prediction_base
        WHERE mrr > 0
    )
    """

    # -- SELECT columns --
    # Always include identity/metadata columns needed by callers
    select_cols = [
        "cpb.customer_id",
        "cpb.event_day",
        "cpb.mrr",
    ]
    if include_target:
        target_col = get_target_column()
        select_cols.append(f"cpb.{target_col}")

    # Feature columns queried from SQL (is_plan_* and is_primary_id_* are derived in Python)
    select_cols.extend([
        "cpb.day_number",
        "GREATEST(0, ccm.day_number_including_trial_days - cpb.day_number) as trial_days",
        "cpb.plan_type",
        "cpb.primary_id_grouping",
        "CASE WHEN mrr.payment_cadence = 'year' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END as is_annual_payment_cadence",
        "ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days",
        "CASE WHEN ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days > 0 THEN 2.0 * ccm.mail_scans_past_15_days::FLOAT / ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days ELSE 1.0 END as scan_velocity_15d_30d",
        "CASE WHEN ccm.mail_scans_past_60_days > 0 THEN 2.0 * ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days::FLOAT / ccm.mail_scans_past_60_days ELSE 1.0 END as scan_velocity_30d_60d",
        "CASE WHEN ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days > 0 THEN 100.0 * ccm.rescans_past_30_days::FLOAT / ccm.mail_scans_past_30_days ELSE 0.0 END as rescan_rate_past_30_days",
        "ccm.check_count_deposited_past_30_days",
        "COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_15_days, 0) as login_days_past_15_days",
        "COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_30_days, 0) as login_days_past_30_days",
        "CASE WHEN COALESCE(ccm.login_days_past_30_days, 0) > 0 THEN 2.0 * ccm.login_days_past_15_days::FLOAT / ccm.login_days_past_30_days ELSE 1.0 END as login_velocity_15d_30d",
        "COALESCE(ccm.login_unique_users_past_15_days, 0) as login_unique_users_past_15_days",
        "COALESCE(ccm.login_unique_users_past_30_days, 0) as login_unique_users_past_30_days",
        "cpb.cumulative_mail_scans",
        "cpb.cumulative_check_count_deposited",
        "ccm.cumulative_check_usd_deposited",
        "cpb.cumulative_registered_agent_locations",
        "cpb.cumulative_completed_1583_flows",
        "ccm.cumulative_routing_rules",
        "ccm.cumulative_users",
        "ccm.cumulative_teams",
        "ccm.cumulative_tags",
        "ccm.cumulative_mailbox_locations",
        "cpb.mrr - COALESCE(mrr_lag.mrr, 0) as mrr_change_past_30d",
        f"CASE WHEN cpb.event_day >= DATE '{PRICE_INCREASE_START}' - INTERVAL '{PREDICTION_HORIZON_WEEKS} weeks' AND cpb.event_day < DATE '{PRICE_INCREASE_END}' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END as {get_repricing_feature_name()}",
    ])

    select_clause = ",\n        ".join(select_cols)

    # -- FROM / JOIN --
    cross_join = "\n    CROSS JOIN latest_day" if latest_day_only else ""

    from_clause = f"""FROM public.view_churn_prediction_base cpb
    JOIN public.view_customer_cumulative_metrics ccm
        ON cpb.customer_id = ccm.customer_id
        AND cpb.event_day = ccm.event_day
    JOIN public.view_mrr_by_customer_by_day mrr
        ON cpb.customer_id = mrr.customer_id
        AND cpb.event_day = mrr.event_day
    LEFT JOIN public.view_mrr_by_customer_by_day mrr_lag
        ON cpb.customer_id = mrr_lag.customer_id
        AND cpb.event_day - 30 = mrr_lag.event_day{cross_join}"""

    # -- WHERE --
    # Data quality filters applied consistently at both training and prediction time:
    where_conditions = [
        # Only paying customers (trials excluded)
        "cpb.mrr > 0",
        # Exclude "early custom switch" observations: customers who temporarily started on
        # grow/scale while their custom contract was being set up. day_number_for_nrr < 0
        # during this period, and their MRR doesn't reflect the true contract value.
        "mrr.day_number_for_nrr >= 0",
        # Exclude 'no_base_product': customers with addon MRR but no base plan,
        # typically a transient billing setup issue.
        "cpb.plan_type != 'no_base_product'",
    ]

    if include_target:
        # Exclude rows where the target hasn't been observed yet. The view
        # populates mrr_in_N_weeks as NULL when future data doesn't exist.
        # For 4-week horizon this is a no-op (view already trims ~4 weeks),
        # but for 8- or 12-week horizons there's a gap of rows with NULL targets.
        target_col = get_target_column()
        where_conditions.append(f"cpb.{target_col} IS NOT NULL")

    if date_start:
        where_conditions.append(f"cpb.event_day >= '{date_start}'")
    if date_end:
        where_conditions.append(f"cpb.event_day < '{date_end}'")
    if latest_day_only:
        where_conditions.append("cpb.event_day = latest_day.max_day")

    where_clause = "\n      AND ".join(where_conditions)

    return f"""{cte}SELECT
        {select_clause}
    {from_clause}
    WHERE {where_clause}
    ORDER BY cpb.event_day, cpb.customer_id
    """


# =============================================================================
# S3 CONFIGURATION
# =============================================================================

S3_BUCKET = "stable-ml-models"
S3_MODEL_PREFIX = "nrr-prediction-model"
S3_PREDICTIONS_PREFIX = "nrr-predictions"
